posts.Title: The Hidden Math Behind AI Layoffs: It's Not About Replacement
posts.Body:
I've been obsessively reading layoff announcements for months. Call it morbid curiosity, but I needed to understand what's really happening in the job market.

Here's what I found: Most companies aren't saying "AI can do Jennifer's job better." They're saying "We need Jennifer's salary to pay for AI tools."

The math is brutal when you break it down.

One $60,000 salary equals:
• 5,000 months of ChatGPT Plus
• 2+ years of enterprise AI subscriptions
• Months of cloud computing for AI training
• A team of virtual assistants and automation tools

This hit me last week when my company announced "strategic restructuring to invest in emerging technologies." Translation: cutting people to fund AI infrastructure.

But here's the thing everyone's missing — this isn't about AI being better at your job yet. It's about AI being cheaper than your job right now.

The companies making these cuts aren't necessarily seeing AI outperform humans. They're seeing AI tools that cost $20/month do 60% of what they paid someone $5,000/month to do.

For many businesses, 60% efficiency at 0.4% of the cost is an easy decision.

So the real question isn't "Can AI do what I do?" It's "Am I generating enough value to justify my cost when AI tools are getting this cheap?"

The answer, for most of us, better be building skills that make us force multipliers — not just task completers. Managing AI tools, not competing with them. Creating strategy, not just executing it.

Because the math isn't changing. If anything, AI tools are getting cheaper while salaries stay expensive.

What are you doing to become irreplaceable rather than just... costly?

posts.Title: Why Companies Are Laying Off 30,000 Workers to Fund AI (And What It Means for You)
posts.Body:
I noticed something unsettling while scrolling through LinkedIn last week.

The same companies announcing massive layoffs were simultaneously posting dozens of new job openings. Same week. Sometimes same department.

So I spent three days digging into this pattern, and what I found explains exactly why 2024 feels so unstable for so many people.

Companies aren't just cutting costs. They're reshuffling their entire workforce around AI capabilities. The 30,000 people getting laid off aren't being replaced by robots. They're being replaced by smaller teams of people who know how to use AI as a productivity multiplier.

I tracked 200 job postings from companies that announced layoffs in the past 60 days. 73% of the new roles explicitly mentioned AI tools in the requirements section. Not as a bonus. As a requirement.

Here's what's actually happening: A marketing team of 8 people who don't use AI is getting replaced by a team of 3 people who can use ChatGPT, Claude, and automation tools to produce the same output. The math is simple. Three salaries plus AI subscriptions cost less than eight salaries.

The people getting hired right now fall into two categories: those building AI systems, and those who've learned to work alongside them effectively.

I started learning AI tools four months ago because I could see this shift coming. Not because I love technology, but because I have bills to pay. Now I finish content projects in 2 hours that used to take me 8 hours. My manager noticed. My value doubled.

Meanwhile, my coworker who refuses to "rely on AI" just got her hours cut.

This isn't about replacing human creativity or judgment. It's about amplifying it. The companies making these cuts aren't eliminating the work. They're consolidating it among people who can do it faster and better with AI assistance.

If you're feeling anxious about job security right now, the solution isn't to avoid AI tools. It's to learn them before your company decides you're redundant.

The shift is already here. The question is which side of it you want to be on.
